Original Text:
As the 20th century began, cars like the Model T Ford revolutionized travel. Now, a crucial question arises: are we overly reliant on automobiles? Reducing our dependence on cars is, in my opinion, essential.

Elizabeth Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," underscores the importance of this. Cars dominate urban landscapes from Shanghai to Chicago. Consequently, experts find it challenging to mitigate pollution from vehicles. Shockingly, cars contribute to 12% of Europe's pollution, but this figure skyrockets to 50% in the U.S. due to our excessive car usage! Furthermore, Robert Duffer's piece, "Paris bans driving due to smog," vividly illustrates the environmental harm caused by cars, as Paris prohibited driving to combat smog.

Conversely, Andrew Selsky's article, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," offers a glimmer of hope. Millions of Colombians participated in a car-free day, opting for walking, biking, or public transportation, resulting in remarkably quiet streets. Annually, this event bans personal cars while allowing buses and taxis, significantly improving air quality. Additionally, Bogota has undergone substantial transformations, incorporating parks, recreational spaces, sidewalks, and commercial establishments.

Ultimately, diminishing car usage and promoting car-free initiatives are highly beneficial, leading to cleaner air and more pleasant cities. Bogota's car-free day demonstrates our capacity to reduce traffic congestion and enhance urban living. If we adopted similar measures in America, such as encouraging biking and walking for shorter journeys, we could foster a brighter future. In conclusion, advocating for reduced car use is both a judicious and commendable approach.

Original Text:
A solid transportation system is a must-have for any country; that’s why cars and trains are so essential for travel. Yet, all those cars create big messes, and everywhere we see pollution levels climb! The increased traffic is really ruining our air quality. Finding fast solutions to cut pollution is key.

Take the German town of Vauban, which is actually pretty neat. Not many residents there use cars. Since most shops and important locations are nearby, travel is easier. Although cars are still present, many Vauban residents don't even own one. Believe it or not, over half the families sold their cars upon moving there. They say it's less stressful since less driving is nice. These days, cars account for 12% of all pollution; however, in packed US cities, it might be as high as 50%.

So, Paris was really smoggy, so they tried something radical. No even-numbered license plates were allowed on Mondays. Enormous fines would be handed out if the rule was broken. The next day, it was the odd numbers' turn. Traffic decreased by 60% in just five days. Diesel cars, which are so popular in France, really worsened the smog. But, the ban wasn't permanent because they stopped the every-other-day thing.

Envision a car-free day like in Bogota, Colombia. One day a year, only taxis and buses are permitted. Pollution drops significantly on this day, and they've been doing it since the 90s. Later, they added bike paths, parks, and relaxation spots. Giving up cars might be difficult, but it helps the environment.

Look, we probably won’t get rid of cars completely soon, but countries need to try to reduce pollution and make driving less attractive. Taking action to combat air pollution will make life better. Even though we use cars a lot, too much driving is bad for us and the world.

Is America moving away from cars? Elisabeth Rosenthal poses an interesting question. It seems fewer people are driving, which is fantastic! Think about it: fewer crashes, healthier lives, and cleaner air simply because we're not driving as much. Do we even need cars the way we used to?

Firstly, driving less drastically improves health and safety, which is key, right? Andrew Selsky recalled the peaceful streets of Colombia on a car-free day. Fewer cars, therefore less stress! Traffic jams ruin our days and make us irritable. Thus, less driving, less traffic, less stress! Heidrun Walter felt happier and less stressed after selling her car. Moreover, we get more exercise. Cars are handy, but we forget to be active. Selsky noted numerous new parks and sports areas. Fewer cars could lead to more parks, inspiring us to move around. Essentially, less driving equals less stress and healthier living!

Original Text:
Respected Senator,

I'm reaching out about a big change: how we pick our president. We should really think about getting rid of the Electoral College. We need a fairer system where everyone's vote counts right away! Right now, it's old and unfair; electors can go against what most people want – that's not okay!

Sure, changing the Electoral College won't be easy. But it's worth it in the long run. If we just counted everyone's votes, candidates would have to try to get support from everywhere. We'd also need to figure out what to do if there's a tie, to keep things fair. So, even if we can't totally get rid of the Electoral College, we have to fix what's wrong with it.

Right now, electors have way too much say in who wins. The "winner-takes-all" thing in the Electoral College makes voters feel like their votes don't matter. Candidates only spend time and money in a few "swing" states and ignore everyone else. Remember in 2000, when they basically ignored 17 states? Because of stuff like this, the Electoral College is old news and needs to be changed or scrapped.

Candidates pick electors on purpose to help them win. These electors get support without the candidate having to go to every state. Basically, the candidate just waits to see what happens. A candidate can win without even trying that hard, because the electors make the final call. This could lead to shady stuff like bribes in states that matter a lot – that's really scary.

When we vote, we're not actually voting for the candidate. We're voting for electors, which is weird. They get picked in different ways, like at state meetings or by the candidates themselves. One big problem is that you never know what might happen with the Electoral College. Back in 1960, some people in Louisiana even thought about replacing the Democratic electors with people who didn't like John F. Kennedy.

Basically, the Electoral College is a relic from the past. It gives electors too much power and messes with our democratic rights. Maybe it made sense a long time ago, but it doesn't fit with what democracy is all about today.

Original Text:
From the very beginning here in the United States, the Electoral College was written into the Constitution. It makes sense to keep it, simply because it has worked so far. Why mess with something that isn't broken? Sticking with the Electoral College seems smart. Here's why, in five quick points.

Okay, sure, there *are* some possible problems with the Electoral College. For example, the electors might just go rogue and vote how *they* want. Think about it: we aren't really voting for the president; we're voting for electors who then make the call. They could, in theory, ignore what the public wants and do their own thing. Then there is the chance that a state submits conflicting slates of electors; that is a complete mess waiting to happen. Plus, even though it's rare, the Electoral College *could* result in a tie. The "winner-takes-all" thing can also make candidates skip over some states where one party always wins. But with a direct vote, they'd probably try harder to get votes everywhere. Original Text:
Okay, so, the Electoral College? It’s got to stay. Sure, it's old, but picking a President this way is still easiest. Counting every single vote makes elections way harder. Recounts? Fewer of 'em! And it keeps the big states from calling all the shots, which is good. It keeps things from getting totally out of hand.

The Electoral College helps us avoid those messy fights. You often see candidates getting way more electoral votes than actual votes. Take Obama, for example. He snagged almost 62% of electoral votes, even though just about 51% of folks voted for him. States use "winner-take-all," making figuring out electoral votes easy, even if someone barely wins. A tie? Yeah, possible, but super unlikely. But counting every single vote? That's just asking for chaos.

With the Electoral College, we don't have to do runoff elections. These happen when nobody gets over half the votes. Nixon in '68 and Clinton in '92? They only got around 43% of the popular vote. Still, they won big, with 301 and 370 votes respectively. So we get a clear winner without having to vote again. And nobody wants to waste time with runoff votes.

So, the Electoral College? It stops those big states from being too powerful. We gotta keep it around. Maybe there's something better out there, but, for now, it’s the best system we've got.

*Click!* Mom just snapped a pic of my face with her iPhone. Holding that little gadget felt like I had superpowers. Ah, youth! I can still picture myself sprawled on the kitchen floor, pushing a toy car while Mom cooked; the garlic smell was everywhere. And then, finally, my driver's license! A huge deal for us teens, right? But let's be real, it’s not all sunshine and roses. Less driving means less pollution, which means everyone gets to breathe easier.

Now that I'm driving, I can't stand the smog that makes me squint on my way to school. Ugh, and my car's making it even worse! Car fumes create smog, and that sends greenhouse gasses into the air. Shockingly, U.S. cars are responsible for around half of those emissions (Source 1). And get this—even making gasoline pollutes! It's such a waste, releasing tons of carbon. So, the fuel-making process emits greenhouse gasses, and THEN our cars pump out even MORE?! AP Environmental Science hammered home that too many greenhouse gasses are a NO-GO. They trap sunlight and UV rays, leading to global warming. Plus, smog is just plain nasty. Paris was choking on it, so they banned cars to clear the air. And guess what? In just one car-free day, the smog vanished! People were breathing freely again (Source 2). Smog also hurts Paris' tourism—who wants to see Versailles covered in pollution?

Driving less does wonders for our bodies and minds. Take Vauban, Germany, where cars aren't allowed. Loads of people who moved there ditched their cars and are now happier! Heidrun Walker, a media trainer and mom of two, said, "When I had a car, I was stressed. Now, I'm happier." (Source 1). Walker's story shows that saying goodbye to your car can seriously boost your mental well-being. And your body reaps the rewards too! People are walking, running, biking, or skating all over. Bogota, Colombia, has a "Day Without Cars" every year, encouraging people to walk (Source 3). Walking is so great for your heart! If more places had car-free days, maybe we'd see fewer heart problems.

So, less driving equals cleaner air, healthier bodies, and happier minds. Next time you're reaching for your keys to drive to your friend's place, just pause for a sec. Think about your health, the planet, and consider walking instead.

Alright, so the Electoral College, how *exactly* does it work? The Federal Register states this: We vote for *electors*. Then, these *individuals* meet and select the President and Vice President. Many people want to abolish the Electoral College and simply use the popular vote system. Bradford Plumer questions whether electors will even vote as we *expect* them to. And he doubts it! Perhaps sometimes, but we still pick electors and *hope* they choose correctly. We *must* trust them. However, if we voted *directly* for the President, we would *still* have to trust that they would be *competent*. It is basically the same, but with an *additional step* included, don't you think?

He believes the Electoral College maintains fairness, similar to how the Senate aids small states. *Precisely*! Large states have little influence in the Senate. Thus, with the popular vote, large states gain *even MORE* attention because *that* is where the majority of people reside.

Additionally, the Electoral College helps stabilize things if no one receives more than 50% of the votes. Like in '68! Nixon received the *most* votes (43%), but not a majority. Clinton had a similar situation in '92. However, they won the Electoral College, so there was no runoff. That was *close indeed*!

I support the Electoral College. It prevents Texas from dominating Rhode Island. Moreover, it helps us avoid runoffs. Some argue it is outdated, but I believe the Electoral College is a *reliable* voting method.

Look, as someone young, the Electoral College feels ancient. Though I can't vote yet, my thoughts matter too, right? I believe it should stay. If we chose the President solely by popular vote, it'd be a huge mess quickly. Certain regions might adore a candidate, but the rest might dislike them entirely. That'd be a complete disaster.

Choosing a leader only by most votes could be a really awful idea. Article three, paragraph 20, the "winner-take-all" rule, forces candidates to focus on key states. Because these states decide the election, people there are well-informed. However, with just a popular vote, anyone over 18 could vote for dumb reasons. Young folks might back whoever their friends like, or just because they like the party. People might not consider their choices deeply, but the Electoral College stops that.

Also, a popular vote might elect someone known only in one part of the country. Article three, paragraph 19, says a President popular in just one area wouldn't be a good leader, as one area can't elect someone alone. Imagine if popularity was all that mattered. Like, Rodney is super popular in the South, but since he doesn't need more electoral votes, he doesn’t need to visit the South. If that’s all it was, voters elsewhere might feel ignored, and the President wouldn't care about them. He’d still not be “their president,” even if he won. The Electoral College fixes this. We vote for electors who then vote for the president. Winning the most votes in a state means its electors support them; that's fair. A popular vote unfairly hurts people who didn't vote for the winner.

I know some disagree. Article two says, "Candidates don’t visit states they can’t win... Seventeen states didn't even see the candidates in 2000!" Candidates skip states they don’t think they can win. I see the case for the popular vote, where candidates would visit every state. But, I disagree. A candidate visiting all 50 states doesn't guarantee they'll win. The Electoral College makes sure a state's majority matters, not just overall popularity. It’s like high school: the popular kid might be a jerk, while some less popular kids are nice. Choosing a President based on popularity could ruin our country.

Mr. State Senator, the Electoral College matters a lot. A popular vote could give us a bad president or one who wins just because they are popular in one area. Please consider my thoughts when you discuss this.

Original Text:
Perhaps scrapping the Electoral College isn't the greatest idea, no? The Founding Fathers created it so both Congress and regular folks could chime in. Now, folks grumble, claiming it's unfair because the electors are the real decision-makers, not us. We simply vote for them. But electors flipping their votes? Extremely rare, it's just chatter. So, despite the gripes, the Electoral College should remain. It simplifies matters, reduces vote counts, and amplifies the power of smaller states.

Alright, the Electoral College can be a bit of a mess, like in the year 2000, but it doesn't happen nearly as often as arguing about who scored the most votes overall. The reality is, a person can snag the Electoral College victory without winning the popular vote. Take 2012: Obama snagged 61.7% of the electoral vote but only 51.3% of the popular vote. When a candidate wins big in a state, even a narrow win translates into something huge.

Moreover, the Electoral College prevents elections where no one secures over half the votes. Consider 1968 and 1992, when the victors only held around 43% of the vote. That could lead to stalemates and endless voting! Yet, the Electoral College just picks someone right away. Some even think it discourages folks from not voting in states where their candidate is bound to lose, such as Democrats in Texas or Republicans in California. If it's futile, why bother showing up at all?

Despite all this, the Electoral College levels the playing field, offering smaller states a chance against the big boys. In 2012, Florida was neck and neck, and its 29 votes went to Obama. Candidates put in extra effort in larger states because they carry more weight in the Electoral College. People moan about the winner-takes-all situation, but candidates zero in on swing states rather than squandering time where the winner is already a foregone conclusion.

Sure, some might skip voting, figuring their vote doesn't hold much sway, but one vote almost never determines an election's outcome. People vote to voice their beliefs, not because they think they're the sole reason someone wins. The Electoral College, not just the popular vote, should still select our president; plain and simple.

Basically, most suburbs are cookie-cutter, right? But all those cars are a pollution machine. Vauban fixed this by making driving a pain. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote an article called "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars." The article highlights that cars are responsible for about 12% of Europe's greenhouse gasses. But, in some U.S. areas, it's closer to 50%! Vauban helps clean up the air by prioritizing walkways and shops over roads, making it easier to get around on foot. The money saved from skipping road construction goes to other transportation options. Rosenthal's article mentions that typically, 80% of transportation funds go to highways. Only 20% is left for everything else.

Paris is really pushing for a change in how people use cars. There's so much to see in the city, and honestly, you can get almost anywhere on a bike. So, why pump more pollution into the city by driving? That's why Paris sometimes puts a temporary stop to cars. The pollution in Paris is nearly as bad as in Beijing, China (which everyone knows is polluted). Robert Duffer wrote "Paris Bans Driving Due To Smog." Originally, Paris considered a complete car ban to kill off pollution. But, that wasn't going to fly because so many people rely on cars. So, they found a middle ground. On certain days, cars with license plates ending in an even number were off-limits. The next day, it was the turn of cars with odd-numbered plates. This went on for a whole week. Drivers who broke the rules had to cough up 22 euros. Duffer's article pointed out that nearly 4,000 drivers paid the fine that week! Even with the fines, there was a 60% drop in traffic!

Bogota, Colombia, also puts limits on driving. It's a city with almost seven million people living there. Every year, they have a car-free day. People walk, bike, skate, and hop on buses. Andrew Selsky wrote "CarFree Day Is Spinning Into A Big Hit In Bogota." The article explains that the city wants to cut down on pollution, traffic, and accidents by encouraging people to use taxis, buses, and other ways to get around. After three years of success, Cali and Valledupar (two other cities in Colombia) jumped on board. Selsky says the car-free day was part of a plan to make Bogota a better place, starting back in the mid-1990s. As a result of this plan, they built 118 miles of bike paths! Plus, new parks and sports facilities.

Even in the U.S. (where we love our cars), things might be changing. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote "The End Of Car Culture." She mentions that in the past 8 years, fewer young people are bothering to get their licenses and drive. Instead, they're walking, biking, taking the bus, or using ride-sharing services. With traffic getting worse and worse, these options are often faster than driving. And when people do drive, they're carpooling more often. This all helps to reduce air pollution.

These electors subsequently cast their votes for the President and Vice President, after which the votes are tallied. Given the unpopularity of the Electoral College, many perceive a direct vote as a superior method for electing the President and Vice President.

Within each state, each candidate maintains their own roster of electors, typically chosen by their respective party. Discontentment with these electors may lead individuals to perceive their vote as inconsequential, thereby diminishing their perceived influence on the outcome of the presidential election.

In essence, widespread discontent with the Electoral College prevails, owing to its perceived complexity and time-consuming nature. Suspicions arise that officials perpetuate its existence to safeguard their own positions. A direct election has the potential to rectify this situation and facilitate a more streamlined process for selecting our nation's leader.

Original Text:
Does the Electoral College even matter anymore? It helped long ago when states struggled to count votes. People voted by state, and that was acceptable. But now, with better tech, it feels slow and unfair. If you're a Republican in a heavily Democratic state, your vote seems meaningless. Wouldn't a national vote where everyone's voice counts be better? Plus, some states have too much power due to the winner-takes-all system. Small states don't get enough attention, making it hard to decide who to vote for.

Back in the day, when the telegraph was brand new, things were different! Instead of states counting votes and sending people, they needed another way. Imagine getting votes by horse! Too risky! Papers could be lost, people could get hurt, and some places might not get counted. The Electoral College made sense then. But like the telegraph, it's outdated. Now, phones can hold many votes. Computers can quickly do math, giving us fast answers. We can send votes quickly and fix mistakes. What if a computer counted everything? Each place sends info to one computer, with checks and balances. Use multiple computers to prevent cheating. It's a shame some people won't let go of old ways! So outdated and ineffective!

It's just not right. A Democrat votes in a strong Republican area, but the Republican always wins. That Democrat's vote is as if it never existed. Gone. Every time. But a national vote would make those votes count. They would matter! The winner-takes-all system makes it worse. Winning California by a bit means getting ALL their votes, ignoring what half the people think! Unfair. Big states dominate due to more votes. What about the small states? Not much attention. Politicians prefer big states, ignoring the small ones. Voters in places like the Dakotas might not know much and just pick a party. The main battles are in big states. The ads aren't always true, but voters figure it out. Swing states get lots of info. Small states? Forgotten, so voters don't know as much, and their votes might not matter. A national vote means candidates go everywhere, no more big-state bias, and fair voting for all.

So, here's the deal: The Electoral College was fine back then, but now? It's old and unfair. We can easily talk and travel, making it pointless and bad for our democracy. It's time for a change!

Original Text:
Across the world, folks are ditching their cars more and more often. This is great for nature and your wallet. Lots of people want to do their part, even if it's just in their own town. Countries like China, Spain, and the U.S. are seeing fewer people using cars these days. Some might not like it, but others are happy because of all the yucky pollution. Just think, if everyone cut their driving in half, the air would be so much cleaner! Sure, cars are handy, but they cause a whole bunch of problems.

Check out Vauban, Germany, where you barely see any cars. You can own a car, but parking is a real pain and super expensive. It could cost you up to $40,000 just to park near your own place! So, most people park their cars outside the area. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote a piece called "Life Goes On Without Cars in German Suburb," saying that about 70% of families in Vauban don't even own a car. Plus, over half the people who moved there sold their cars! Cutting back on driving can have good and bad sides. But, you can save a lot of money on car payments, parking tickets, and pricey gas. And hey, the air gets cleaner too.

Paris, France, had some serious pollution problems, so they had to ban some cars. Robert Duffer said that cars with license plates ending in even numbers couldn't drive for a bit. If you did, it was a €22 fine. The next day, they banned odd-numbered plates. This really helped cut down on pollution! That's awesome, especially for a big city. The main idea was to make the air better so people could breathe easier. Duffer pointed out that "4,000 drivers got fined" and they "impounded 27 cars." Traffic went down by 60%! Once the air got better, they lifted the ban.

Bogota, Columbia, started with a small thing that blew up big time. Andrew Selsky wrote that "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota." It started small, but now everyone loves it.

Once a year, no one in the city can use their cars! You can still use buses and taxis, though. Around seven million people are trying to make the air better. The goal is to have cleaner ways to get around with way less pollution. If you break the rules, you get a fine! Selsky mentions that Bogota has "118 MILES of bicycle paths!" Parks are nicer, sports are greener, and the air is fresher. It's so popular that other cities are doing it too.

Rosenthal also talked about the U.S. She said that "Recent studies suggest that Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less and getting fewer licenses." Rosenthal 29. This lines up with "Obama's ambitious goals to curb the United States' greenhouse gas emissions!" Rosenthal 29. In New York City, you can easily grab a taxi or hop on the subway. Parking is a nightmare, so people don't want to own cars. Think of all the cash you'd save! Traffic is still crazy, but imagine how much worse it would be with even more cars. It shows how much the city depends on public transit. But public transport can be pricey, and without it, the air would be terrible.

So, cutting back on car use has tons of perks. It fights pollution, makes the air cleaner, and saves you money. If you can, walking is a great choice for your health, your bank account, and the planet. Sure, driving can be faster sometimes, but think about the costs and the pollution. Try using your car less and see the good stuff that happens.

To stop tyranny and give power to the people, the U.S. HAS to keep the Electoral College.

The US presidential election system, the Electoral College, is a controversial topic. Public opinion polls indicate that approximately 60% of Americans favor a direct popular vote system. However, there are proponents who defend the Electoral College. A significant concern is that a candidate can secure the presidency without winning the popular vote. This raises questions about the system's overall fairness.

Electoral votes are allocated to each state based on population size, ranging from 3 to 55. A candidate requires 270 of the 538 electoral votes to win the election. Most states employ a "winner-take-all" system, where the candidate with the most votes in a state receives all of its electoral votes, with Maine and Nebraska being exceptions. In states with consistent voting patterns, the Electoral College can lead to voter apathy. Supporters of the Electoral College argue that it ensures a decisive victory and prevents candidates from focusing solely on densely populated urban areas. Furthermore, it serves as a safeguard in the event of a close popular vote.

Despite its initial purpose of protecting smaller states, the Electoral College no longer effectively serves this function. Candidates prioritize campaigning in swing states to maximize their electoral vote count. States with predictable voting patterns are often overlooked due to the presumed outcome. The question arises whether the Electoral College should be retained if it fails to fulfill its intended purpose.

Despite its long-standing presence and typical selection of a winner, the Electoral College may not be the optimal method for electing presidents, and it could even lead to a tie. Some argue that focusing on swing states increases voter engagement due to the increased importance of individual votes. However, eliminating the Electoral College would simply shift the focus of candidates to different regions. The disparity in the value of votes remains a concern, leading many to advocate for the abolition of the Electoral College as the most equitable solution.

A small state wields equal influence to a large state. This is absolutely irrational! Remember the 2000 election? Gore received the most votes overall, yet Bush secured the presidency through the Electoral College. Plumer concludes that "...the electoral college is unfair to voters...swing states..." Attention is given to only a few states, making the system "...unfair, outdated, and irrational..." due to the possibility of ties and misrepresentation.

Proponents of the Electoral College contend that it brings "...Certainty of Outcome...Everyone's President...Swing States...Big States...Avoid RunOff Elections..."(Posner), but these arguments are unconvincing. The 2000 election was far from certain! "Everyone's President" is a misnomer because electors, not the people, cast the deciding votes. Swing states are excessively prioritized, neglecting the concerns of other states. Posner suggests that it benefits larger states: "The Electoral College restores some of the weight in the political balance that large states by population lose by virtue of the malapportionment of the Senate decreed in the Constitution..." However, this fails to address the fundamental issue. Posner also asserts that it "avoids that problem of elections in which no candidate receives a majority of the votes cast," but the focus on swing states undermines fairness.

In conclusion, we must adopt the popular vote. This system is flawed, as demonstrated in the 2000 election. Swing states possess undue influence, and the potential for a tie poses a significant risk. It is inherently unjust, illogical, and biased. Despite any counterarguments, a fair, democratic popular vote remains the only viable solution.

Original Text:
All around the world, cities are trying to cut down on car use. They're creating car-free zones, setting rules about when people can drive, and even having car-free days. Why? A big reason is pollution, especially from old, dirty diesel trucks. Places like Vauban, Germany, and cities like Paris and Bogotá are trying out new ideas, and guess what? It's actually working! This gives us hope.

Get this: In Vauban, most people don't even own cars. There's no street parking, and the "new zone" isn't designed for cars. You can park far outside the area, but there aren't many spots. Want one? Be ready to shell out $40,000 for a garage way out there!

But here's the cool thing: People are loving not having cars. They enjoy hearing bikes and kids playing instead of loud cars driving by. Vauban's a smart plan that's paying off. By 2006, many new residents even sold their cars after moving in!

Vauban is part of Germany's 20-year plan to make walking and biking easy in the suburbs, and to have great bus service. Think about it: around 5,500 people in just one square mile! It's a great car-free suburb. Germany's really trying to make it easy to live without a car. Instead of malls far away by the highway, stores are right on the main streets, super close to homes.

While Germany's pushing for towns where you don't need cars, others still prefer big suburbs with garages, like around New York City. Some folks just like the old ways. But guess what? Vauban's ideas are catching on!

Paris, France, banned certain cars to fight the bad air – even worse than Beijing's! They told drivers with license plates ending in even numbers to stay home. If you broke the rule, you got a 22 euro fine (or $31). The next day, it was the odd-numbered plates that couldn't drive.

Believe it or not, almost 4,000 cars were towed! But check this out: traffic dropped by about 60%! French leaders blamed it on the diesel cars, even though diesel fuel had a tax break. Guess what? Most cars in France use diesel.

Paris has a real problem with dirty air from diesel cars. This ban made things tough for some, like delivery trucks. But it wasn't all bad! Electric cars, hybrids, and cars with 3+ people could still drive. Plus, buses and trains were free. The air got much better, and they ended the ban after just a few days.

Bogotá, Colombia, started "Car-Free Day," hoping other cities would follow. Millions walked, skated, biked, or took the bus. Colombia has done this every year for three years now. Driving without permission costs $25. The city, with seven million people, wants less smog and hopes to get people to travel in different ways.

Even though it rained, more people came out than expected! Two more cities in Colombia joined in for the first time! Leaders from other countries visited Bogotá to see what was happening. They were really impressed, saying that Colombians were changing, and the idea would spread everywhere!

It all began in the mid-1990s. Bogotá created 118 miles of bike paths – the most in Latin America. They also built parks and sports areas. Small sidewalks became big walkways. Car-Free Day helped new stores and restaurants open.

Studies show Americans are buying fewer cars and driving less. This fits with Obama's goals. The US buys a lot of cars, but pollution might be turning people off.

One reason could be the recession. Many Americans couldn't buy new cars, and that's still kind of true. This is making it hard for the big car companies, who are trying to stay afloat.

Ford and Mercedes are changing things up, calling themselves "mobility companies." Now they make different stuff. Fewer young people (16 to 39) are getting their licenses. They are sharing rides, walking, or using buses and trains. A lot of them just don't see the need for a license.

Lots of countries sometimes ban driving to help with pollution. What happens? The air gets cleaner, of course. These countries are tackling big problems that cause even bigger ones, like global warming. Things are changing, and that's good for everyone.

If we didn't have the winner-take-all thing and just did a popular vote, campaigns would be all over the place and a mess. Many people believe it's a hot mess. It fouls up representation, makes people not want to vote, and candidates only pay attention to a handful of states.

The main issue? The Electoral College is simply unjust. States with more people get more "electoral votes." Take Wyoming with its 500,000 voters. Hard to take.

Some argue that giving votes based on population is okay. However, a national popular vote system is way better. The one with the most votes nationwide should win, period. In states where one party dominates, like Republicans in Texas, that party wins. A Texas Democrat might believe their vote does not matter and not vote. This stops voters from voting, which is against what America stands for.

When a state grows, its Electoral College power grows. So, candidates focus on these "big states," focusing on their voters. States with fewer people? They are mostly ignored. Candidates travel and make speeches, but some states do not make the cut. Back in 2000, seventeen states were completely ignored! A lot of people in those states lose interest in voting, but who can blame them?

The Electoral College is complex. It turns voters off, lowers the number of people who vote, and forces candidates to only care about the states with the most people. Switching to a national popular vote would solve these issues and actually make presidential elections better.

It muddles the waters for voters, could even lead to a deadlock, and – get this – we don't even directly vote for the top person!

This whole College idea? Truly, utterly unfair. Candidates only ever seem to focus on the swing states, you know? These states hand EVERYTHING to the winner, which means everyone else? Completely, utterly ignored. Like they're not even there! Back in 2000, seventeen states got absolutely NO attention whatsoever. The media? No ads. Those voters barely got any info to go on! Candidates end up hiding important stuff! No ads, no visits, so actually understanding what you're voting for is super hard! The College is just straight up unfair.

And what if this whole College thing results in a tie? Ugh, total catastrophe. The House gets to decide, and each state only gets one vote. Seriously? The final outcome might not even be what the state actually wants. It all depends on just a tiny few voters, which is so messed up, right? A tie totally ruins the whole election's vibe, doesn't it?

Plus, the bond we have to our president? Severed. We're voting for electors, not the president himself. So, if someone in Texas likes John Kerry? They're backing 34 Dems who are saying they'll vote for him. And anyone can be an elector. Even someone not involved in politics might be biased. This whole College thing? Is so confusing.

Senator, seriously, this College? Just bad news all around. Voters are bummed out, ties are a real, awful possibility, and the election isn't even direct. Voters just feel like something's wrong. A tie? Absolutely horrible. Electors, not the president! It’s just wrong, outdated, and a headache.

Original Text:
Thinking about ditching your car? Awesome move! It's a choice that comes with some seriously cool perks: you'll be helping the planet, keeping more cash in your pocket, and dodging those rage-inducing traffic snarls.

One HUGE benefit? You're doing Mother Earth a solid. Elizabeth Rosenthal points out that cars spew a TON of greenhouse gases into the atmosphere. In Europe, we're talking about 12% of the total. But, in the US, that number can skyrocket to a whopping 50%! All that nasty pollution creates smog, especially in our cities. Robert Duffer even chats about how Paris is trying to ban cars and fight smog. Some are even dreaming of suburbs without cars! The payoff: less pollution and fresher air for everyone.

Fewer cars equals less traffic! Check out Bogota, Colombia. They had a "no car day" to cut down on pollution and make traffic flow smoother. Less gridlock means less stress and easier commutes. Say goodbye to tardiness! People walked, biked, or hopped on the bus. Bogota even threw in some new parks and sports fields. So, fewer cars mean healthier folks and a healthier planet.

And get this: there are money-saving advantages too! You'll buy less gas, and those car expenses just vanish. Your road, parking, and street light taxes will shrink. Less dough gets burned on roads and garages. City dwellers already use subways and buses to save a buck. Cutting back on car use is a super easy way to shrink pollution.

Bottom line, driving less is a win-win-win! It's a kindness to the planet, untangles traffic, gets you moving, and pads your wallet. It might be trickier in the countryside, but it's a fantastic option if you're living in the city!

Original Text:
Cars are super common, aren't they? I mean, they're basically everywhere you look across the globe. Folks need them for family visits, to go to work, and, well, to just get around their town and such. But, guess what? ditching cars is becoming the cool thing in some European and US cities. Take Vauban, Germany – it's almost car-free! Heidrun Walter, a local mom, says, "I was stressed with a car; now I'm happier." Who knew it was possible and might make you feel better?

Listen up – getting rid of cars is worth the hassle. Cars actually cause around 12% of Europe's greenhouse gasses and a crazy 50% in the US! So, driving less means less pollution – which is awesome. Remember Paris? They banned cars due to bad smog, only allowing some on certain days. Traffic went down 60%, and the air was cleaner! Imagine if every city did that – how great would that be?

But going car-free isn't the only option, you know. Heard of Bogota, Colombia? They have a yearly car-free day that everyone loves. For three years, they got rid of cars (except for buses and taxis), so folks could try other travel ways and lower smog in the city of seven million. If you break the rules, you get fined! Also, Cali and Valledupar tried it out, too! Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, stated, "It's great to clean the air." A bit of time can make a real difference.

Guess what's happening in America? Maybe they're ditching cars. Vehicle purchases are down, driving is down, and license rates have fallen. Doug Short from Advisor Perspectives said that we drove the most back in 2005, and it's been going down ever since. In 2013, driving fell almost 9% from the peak. It's looking like Americans just don't need as many cars now. It helps cut carbon emissions, which is great for the environment. There are now many transport ways, such as NYC's bike sharing and San Francisco's BART. It turns out there are tons of options to try.

Finally, ditching the car and opting for different transport ways is a total win! Going green is way easier when you drive less. So many cool choices exist, and the good definitely wins over the bad.

The Electoral College, in my view, is a solid system for picking our presidents; it's valuable for many reasons. A major benefit lies in its method of distributing power across states, guaranteeing a clear victor. Maintaining a strict election timeline is another essential feature it upholds. Electors selected through this process often exhibit a keen understanding of public desires. Crucially, it ensures everyone has a say and that everything runs smoothly.

Consider this: Big states such as Florida boast a substantial 29 votes, while smaller ones like Wyoming have just 3. The sway of a state hinges on its population size, reflecting the number of senators and representatives. Irrespective of population, each state is entitled to a minimum of three votes, which feels pretty reasonable to me. This prevents larger states from dominating smaller ones. Should Wyoming's population surge, its vote count could increase, while Florida's might diminish. Florida's abundant votes reflect its diverse population. This reality forces voters to contemplate choices. Imagine the scenario without the Electoral College, and California consistently winning due to its massive population.

Ties are rare, thanks to the winner-takes-all system in most states. Swing states play a pivotal role, with candidates focusing their attention on states where victory is attainable rather than those they are sure to lose. They rely on their supporters, too. It is just like when you're shopping and you only buy what you actually need! Observing where candidates travel becomes highly informative.

Presidential inaugurations should always be on January 20th or March 4th. A consistent timeline is essential, a feat that would be challenging to achieve without the Electoral College. It keeps elections tidy and punctual, normally on the second Thursday of November. Just imagine how chaotic it would become to do away with the Electoral College and recount every single vote. Think of the months and months it could take in Texas alone! We'd be without a leader, causing a real mess.

As a longstanding feature of American democracy, the Electoral College would be difficult to change. This tradition should be respected. It shares power, ensures a clear winner, and keeps elections on schedule. Ultimately, this approach establishes order, which benefits everyone.

It's strange that we don't directly elect the president, and electors could switch sides. Picture a state or even one elector flipping an election! Makes democracy seem wobbly, doesn't it?

Despite some defenses, the Electoral College brings problems. Politicians chase big states with lots of electoral votes, leaving smaller ones out. For instance, a candidate might camp out in California for its 55 votes, while ignoring Montana's small 3. It feels like grabbing electoral votes matters more than helping everyone. Elections seem focused on a few big states, not the whole nation.

Like in "The Indefensible Electoral College," aren't we choosing people to choose the president? "Electors Election," not "Presidential Election"! Electors should follow instructions, but sometimes don't. Imagine if one suddenly changed their mind – election chaos! Democracy should reflect the people's will, not just electors. Some say, "If it's not broken, don't fix it," pointing to the Electoral College's age. But shouldn't we improve by checking our systems? Where would we be if we didn't question things? We need to watch the Electoral College to ensure it really reflects the people's presidential choice.

Fundamentally, the Electoral College seems to favor electoral power over the American people's desires. Some states have too much influence, and electors have excessive power. If we want a government that cares about everyone, we must act on the Electoral College. Our goal is for it to represent the people's will, so we can advance as a nation.

Every day, people hop in their cars for work, errands, or fun, often overlooking the greenhouse gases harming Earth. This everyday pollution is a major concern, particularly in urban areas. That's why countries and organizations have launched initiatives to encourage less driving, which has significantly decreased harmful pollution and improved quality of life!

One major benefit of reducing car use is the decrease in pollution and greenhouse gases. Driving less allows individuals to save money on fuel while also contributing to environmental protection. Elisabeth Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," highlights the substantial contribution of cars to greenhouse gas emissions. In Europe, cars account for 12% of these emissions, whereas in the US, they contribute a staggering 50%! These figures underscore the significant impact of cars on gas emissions. Envision the improvements if more cities adopted strategies to reduce car dependence. Comparing greenhouse gas levels in car-free zones with those in car-heavy areas reveals a striking contrast, with notably lower pollution levels in the absence of cars.

Furthermore, fewer cars have the potential to transform cities for the better. With reduced driving, businesses might relocate to more accessible locations, attracting more patrons. Consequently, similar businesses might emerge nearby. The article "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota" illustrates this point, stating, "Parks and sports centers have appeared, bumpy sidewalks have become wide and smooth, traffic jams have been greatly reduced, and new restaurants and fancy shops have shown up." Reduced car usage can lead to substantial urban improvements!

In conclusion, numerous compelling reasons exist to drive less! From purifying the air to enhancing urban environments, reduced driving can trigger significant, positive transformations.

Original Text:
The clamor to abolish the Electoral College echoes loudly, reminiscent of the year 2000. A multitude deems it unjust, obsolete, and worthy of immediate eradication.

Hold on a moment! Richard A. Posner presented a contrasting perspective. In his view, it forestalls "elections teetering on a razor's edge, where a clear victor is elusive." It delivers a definitive winner, averting protracted and inconclusive runoff elections. Furthermore, Posner astutely observed that the victor typically garners a substantially larger Electoral College margin than the popular vote tally, diminishing the likelihood of a contentious dispute over the outcome compared to a straightforward count of individual votes. Nevertheless, the fundamental principle remains: the people should elect their president. Consequently, the Electoral College appears as a chaotic entanglement, fueling the desire for its elimination.

A prominent concern revolves around the potential for the popular vote winner to be denied victory, thereby thwarting the nation's collective will. Bradford Plumer warns that the Electoral College raises the specter of "the popular vote victor [losing] once more." This erosion of popular sovereignty is detrimental to the foundations of a democratic society. Plumer further elucidates that voters lack complete "control over the votes of their electors," thereby diminishing their influence in determining the election's outcome. This undermines the essence of popular sovereignty, presenting a compelling justification for the prompt abolition of the Electoral College.

An additional dilemma lies in the immense power vested in electors. Plumer emphasizes that "electors retain the capacity to defy the popular will," highlighting their pivotal role in presidential selection. Such power should rightfully reside with the people! He elaborates that electors "have, on occasion, disregarded their party's mandate and cast their votes according to personal preference," illustrating their autonomy. The popular vote should reign supreme. America should adhere to the will of its people, necessitating the direct election of the president.

In summation: The Electoral College perpetuates an inequitable system for Americans. The inviolable right to choose our leader must be safeguarded. The popular vote must prevail, and the abolition of the Electoral College must be achieved definitively.

Original Text:
The United States employs the Electoral College for presidential elections, a scheme conceived from the outset. Your vote effectively selects electors, who then cast their ballots for president and vice president. Congress subsequently tallies these votes. Some back this system, believing it ensures a definitive victor. Conversely, others disapprove, arguing it advantages populous states and can yield peculiar results. This all-encompassing system isn't universally appreciated.

A state's elector count mirrors its congressional representation, implying greater population equates to increased sway. Consider Hawaii, with a mere four electors, contrasted with Texas's significant thirty-eight! Consequently, candidates prioritize voters in major urban centers. Bradford Plumer highlighted Wyoming's "one vote per 500,000 voters, mirroring the impact of 55 individuals in California with 35 million voters. Ergo, the ultimate outcome might diverge from the national preference.”

Electing presidents via this mechanism can be intricate. In 2000, Gore secured more popular votes yet lost the election. Furthermore, can we genuinely rely on electors to honor their commitments? An elector's vote alteration could sway the election's result. Additionally, historically, in 1960, Hawaii submitted two distinct lists of electors.

The winner-take-all approach begets a substantial quandary. Securing the most votes within a state grants you all its electors. Consequently, candidates overlook states they deem unwinnable, concentrating on "swing states." Richard A. Posner observed, "During 2000, 17 states were devoid of candidate visits, such as Rhode Island and South Carolina. Furthermore, residents in 25 prominent media markets were spared campaign advertisements.”

In summary, the Electoral College poses challenges. It introduces complexity and can feel unjust. Even Nixon and Carter advocated for its abolition. Candidates excessively prioritize select states, electors retain the potential to change their votes, and the winner-take-all principle lacks equity. To put it plainly, the Electoral College obscures the process.

Air pollution poses a substantial problem. Rosenthal stated, "Cars create 12% of greenhouse gases in Europe... and in some U.S. cities, it's as high as 50%! P5." Too many cars lead to awful city smog. Think about the Beijing Olympics in '09; reducing car usage quickly cleared the air! This was vital for athletes and tourists, particularly since everyone in Beijing was already wearing masks. It's difficult, but less car use can help prevent terrible issues. Duffer highlights that Paris also reduced pollution, even restricting certain license plates P19! Reduced car pollution equates to cleaner air and more beautiful views!

Interestingly, a large number of young people are choosing not to get driver's licenses, even though they were raised with cars. Selsky points out that fewer cars free up space for parks, sports fields, shops, and pedestrian areas P28! This is also beneficial for new businesses! Rather than malls located by highways, shops are emerging near trains and buses, where walking is convenient. Rosenthal also mentions that his family uses buses, carpools, and walking to decrease their driving P40. Taking the bus or train is an excellent option! Carpooling is also helpful! Thanks to carpools, walking, and buses, exploring and navigating the city has become much easier.

Cities are evolving to support those who opt out of car use. Reducing car dependency brings about many positive changes. With cleaner air and easier commutes, cars are becoming increasingly unnecessary. If we persist in reducing our reliance on cars, the future could be even brighter!

Original Text:
Okay, so electors are picked, and they vote for the president. Then the votes get counted. But, is this good, or could it mess things up big time? We gotta get rid of it! It's not a direct vote from the people, and that could be a problem.

Some say the electoral college stops messy runoff elections; that it makes things easier. Richard A. Posner said that folks might want runoffs in super-close races. So, the idea is, if we keep the electoral college, we won't have runoffs as much. But is that really so great, and what could go wrong? If electors pick the president, can we trust them to prevent runoffs? Even though Posner thinks it makes interference less likely, that tiny plus isn't enough to make up for all the bad stuff.

Imagine everything falling apart. We'd be in deep trouble! The author says we were lucky the 2000 election was only the worst in a hundred years. Next time, it could be way worse. History shows it's happened before. Plumer thinks the electoral college has hurt the U.S., and keeping it could bring more chaos. The author reminds us electors have gone against their party before, Plumer says. So, it doesn't do anything good and lets people mess with the system. It has to go because it could wreck our government.

Also, it's not really a people's vote. The Office of the Federal Register says when you vote for someone, you're voting for *their* electors. So, the people don't directly pick the country's leader. It doesn't seem right for leaders to make choices for people who didn't even vote for them. Plumer says it's unfair, old, and just plain dumb. These problems show how the electoral college hurts people's trust in the government. Plumer also notes that candidates ignore states they can't win and only focus on "swing" states. Winning is more important than what's best for the country. Can we let this ruin us?

Bottom line: the electoral college? Ditch it.

Original Text:
Respected Florida Senator, The founders created the Electoral College, hoping the president would be the people's choice. Yet, it can lead to a victory without the most votes. This old setup raises the risk of ties. Many Americans yearn for true democracy, but this system can hinder it.

The popular vote best reflects the nation's will. Over 60 percent favor direct presidential elections. People want their votes to truly count. When the President sends our troops into harm's way, the average person wants to have their opinions validated with meaningful votes, instead of being disregarded by the Electoral College. Just 538 electors wield much more power than the voice of countless ordinary voters. We are afforded rights like "life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness," so freedom should encompass fair voting.

The Electoral College's longevity is precisely why it presents a problem. The U.S. must modernize its voting laws to stay relevant. As the world evolves, so too should our systems. We should feel fortunate that "The American people should consider themselves lucky that the 2000 fiasco was the biggest election crisis in a century" because history could definitely repeat itself.

Worse still, a tie could occur, making things infinitely more problematic. Someone once noted that "perhaps most worrying is the prospect of a tie in the electoral vote." Democracy should not end in ties. The people's voice, not a stalemate, should determine the victor. Source 2 says, "the election is only a few swing voters away from catastrophe." A tie makes the popular vote irrelevant, which is a genuinely frightening prospect.

In conclusion, we should abolish the Electoral College because it harms the people. It diminishes our votes, it is outdated, and it could trigger a tie. I trust that we elect the president based on the will of the people, rather than the machinations of those in D.C.

Original Text:
Wow, the Electoral College, huh? It's a real hot topic that always sparks debate. Should we keep it, or should we dump it? Some folks think it’s super unfair, messing up the process for both voters and candidates. But others? They insist it’s the best way to choose a president. Given such strong views, is there a way to compromise and make it better?

Instead of just a simple vote count, the Electoral College tries to ensure fairness in presidential elections. Every state has its own set of electors chosen by political parties. So, when you cast your ballot, you're actually supporting the electors who are pledged to the candidate you want in office.

Why the push to get rid of the Electoral College? Well, things can get complicated. A major concern is the potential for a tie. How could that even happen? Sometimes, electors might go rogue and vote as they please. Although we think our votes directly choose electors, they might not always act as we expect. This is a problem if electors abandon their party’s candidate. After Al Gore won the popular vote in 2000 but lost the election, many voters wanted direct elections, which really fueled anger toward the Electoral College.

But there are also strong supporters who want to preserve it. Each party selects electors who are likely to stay loyal to their candidate, and they usually do. Parties prefer to choose individuals who are extremely dedicated. Plus, it highlights the importance of gaining support from different areas of the country. A president who only appeals to a single region might struggle to lead the entire nation effectively. Simply satisfying one group isn't sufficient, making victory challenging. The Electoral College can also be useful when no candidate secures a clear majority.

Ultimately, the debate over whether to keep or abolish the Electoral College reveals valid arguments on both sides. It’s our decision, as citizens, to make.

Original Text:
For families, cars are really useful for getting kids to school and other places. Still, a lot more people are wondering if using cars less could actually help the environment a lot.

We all know cars hurt the planet. They make the air dirty and let out gases that warm the earth, which is a big worry. In Europe, cars cause about 12% of these bad gases. But in the US, where everyone drives, it can be as much as 50%! Bad traffic makes things even worse. Like in France, diesel cars make nasty smog, especially when it's hot. So, less driving means cleaner air for us all.

Also, driving less can make you feel better. People all over have tried living without cars, picking other ways to get around. Heidrun Walkter, a German mom with money, sold her car. Now she loves walking and hearing bikes and kids. She said, "I was always stressed with a car. Now, I feel calmer." Plus, less driving makes you move more. In Colombia, millions joined a day without cars. They walked, biked, or took the bus to fight smog. It worked great! People felt closer and less stressed. One guy, biking with his wife, said it was good for the earth and them.

Basically, driving less is an easy way to get cleaner air, less smog, and more biking. From Germany and France to Colombia and the US, many countries are trying hard to fix the bad stuff that comes with driving.
Humanized Text:

Let's face it, cars aren't exactly eco-friendly. They spew out pollutants, contributing to air pollution and releasing greenhouse gases, a major environmental concern. In Europe, cars are responsible for approximately 12% of these emissions. However, in the car-centric US, that figure can soar to a whopping 50%! And congested traffic just exacerbates the problem. Take France, for instance, where diesel cars generate horrible smog, particularly during heatwaves. Ultimately, fewer cars on the road translate to cleaner air for everyone.

Furthermore, ditching the car can actually boost your well-being. Numerous individuals globally have experimented with car-free living, opting for alternative modes of transportation. Heidrun Walkter, a well-off German mother, sold her car and now revels in walking and the sounds of bikes and children. "I was perpetually stressed with a car," she confessed. "Now, I feel much more relaxed." What's more, reduced driving encourages more physical activity. In Colombia, millions participated in a car-free day, choosing to walk, bike, or take public transport to combat smog. The initiative proved remarkably successful, fostering a sense of community and reducing stress levels. One man, cycling with his wife, remarked on its positive impact on both the environment and their personal well-being.

Essentially, curbing car use offers a straightforward path to cleaner air, reduced smog, and increased cycling. From Germany and France to Colombia and the US, numerous nations are actively striving to mitigate the detrimental effects associated with driving.

Original Text:
Hey Senator, think about backing the idea of picking Presidents by popular vote—it's super important, and I'll tell you why. Just imagine: Electors could totally flip out and ignore what folks want. Plus, we could end up with a total mess, sending the call to the House. And this whole winner-takes-all thing? Totally nuts!

Can you even trust those electors? They've messed up before. Bradford Plumer said some people almost wrecked the '60 election by swapping electors at the last second. Believe it or not, they just vote however they want sometimes, even against their party—talk about crazy!

What if the Electoral College ties? So scary! The House gets to decide, with each state getting one vote. Picture this: tiny Wyoming with the same power as huge California! Back in '76, a few votes changing in Ohio and Hawaii almost caused a tie. That's way too close for comfort.

The winner-take-all setup is just not fair to people. Candidates spend all their money in swing states, forgetting about the rest of us. In 2000, many states got zero love from candidates. A bunch of city folks didn't even see one ad. Are we okay with voters in places like Ohio calling all the shots? Doesn't seem right.

So, Senator, get this: push for a popular vote system when picking Presidents! Because electors could bail on voters, a tie sends stuff to the House, and winner-take-all is just wrong and unfair to the American people.
